content.text: Support Escalation and Knowledge Base Integration

A three-tier support model is only as strong as the paths connecting the tiers and the knowledge infrastructure that allows each tier to work autonomously before escalating. Without a functioning escalation protocol, L1 agents escalate everything to L2 as a risk-minimisation strategy, flooding the technical queue with questions the documentation already answers. Without a maintained knowledge base, L2 agents re-solve the same issues repeatedly instead of building institutional memory. Escalation and knowledge management are the connective tissue that makes the tier model produce compounding returns over time.

attrs.alt: Escalation path and knowledge base feedback loop diagram showing L1/L2/L3 tiers and KB contribution arrows

content.text: An escalation is a transfer of ownership, not a hand-wave. The escalating agent must satisfy three conditions before routing a ticket upward: they have performed the required resolution steps for their tier, they have attached the information the receiving tier needs to continue without starting over, and they have set the customer's expectation about the transition. Escalating because a ticket looks hard is not sufficient. Escalating because the issue is genuinely outside the tier's scope and evidence has been gathered — that is the protocol.

The information handed over in an escalation determines whether the receiving tier can resolve the issue quickly or must spend their first hour re-gathering what L1 already had. A structured escalation packet prevents the information loss that occurs when a ticket is re-routed without context.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import datetime

class EscalationReason(str, Enum):
    OUTSIDE_TIER_SCOPE      = "outside_tier_scope"
    REPRODUCTION_CONFIRMED  = "reproduction_confirmed"   # systematic bug confirmed
    SAFETY_SIGNAL_DETECTED  = "safety_signal_detected"
    DATA_EXPOSURE_SUSPECTED = "data_exposure_suspected"
    ENTERPRISE_SLA_AT_RISK  = "enterprise_sla_at_risk"

@dataclass
class EscalationPacket:
    ticket_id: str
    from_tier: str                  # "L1" or "L2"
    to_tier: str                    # "L2" or "L3"
    reason: EscalationReason
    # What was already tried
    resolution_steps_attempted: list[str]
    kb_articles_checked: list[str]  # KB article IDs consulted
    # Context for the receiving tier
    customer_impact_summary: str    # 1-2 sentences
    reproduction_steps: str | None  # required for L2 → L3
    model_id: str | None
    api_endpoint: str | None
    sample_request_response: str | None  # sanitised — no PII
    escalated_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        if self.from_tier == "L2" and self.to_tier == "L3":
            if not self.reproduction_steps:
                errors.append("L2 → L3 escalation requires reproduction_steps")
            if not self.sample_request_response:
                errors.append("L2 → L3 escalation requires a sanitised sample request/response")
        if not self.resolution_steps_attempted:
            errors.append("resolution_steps_attempted must not be empty")
        return errors

The knowledge base is not a static FAQ. It is a living system that must be updated each time a ticket is resolved that does not have a matching article, and each time an existing article fails to help an agent resolve a ticket. Without a feedback loop from ticket resolution back into the KB, the article library diverges from the actual questions customers are asking and becomes a monument to problems that no longer arise rather than a tool for the ones that do.

Articles are organised by issue category, not by product area, because agents search by symptom rather than by where in the codebase the issue originated. Each article has a freshness score that decays with time and resets when the article is reviewed. An article below the freshness threshold is flagged for review; articles that consistently fail to help agents resolve tickets (measurable by ticket resolution rates when the article was surfaced) are flagged for rewrite or retirement.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class ArticleStatus(str, Enum):
    CURRENT     = "current"
    STALE       = "stale"        # past freshness threshold
    RETIRED     = "retired"      # explicitly retired; no longer surfaced

@dataclass
class KBArticle:
    article_id: str
    title: str
    issue_category: str
    tier_audience: list[str]        # e.g. ["L1"], ["L1", "L2"]
    last_reviewed: date
    FRESHNESS_DAYS = 90             # articles older than 90d without review are stale

    # Usage metrics (updated nightly from ticket resolution data)
    times_surfaced: int = 0
    times_helped_resolve: int = 0   # agent confirmed article helped
    times_flagged_unhelpful: int = 0

    def freshness_status(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> ArticleStatus:
        ref = as_of or date.today()
        if (ref - self.last_reviewed).days > self.FRESHNESS_DAYS:
            return ArticleStatus.STALE
        return ArticleStatus.CURRENT

    def help_rate(self) -> float | None:
        if self.times_surfaced == 0:
            return None
        return self.times_helped_resolve / self.times_surfaced

    def needs_attention(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> list[str]:
        flags = []
        if self.freshness_status(as_of) == ArticleStatus.STALE:
            flags.append(f"Stale: last reviewed {self.last_reviewed}")
        rate = self.help_rate()
        if rate is not None and rate < 0.2 and self.times_surfaced > 20:
            flags.append(f"Low help rate: {rate:.0%} over {self.times_surfaced} surfacings")
        return flags
KB Gap Detection and New Article SLA

A KB gap is detected when an agent resolves a ticket and cannot link it to any existing article. The agent flags the ticket as a gap at close. Flagging is the mandatory last step of the resolution workflow — the ticket form does not allow closure without either linking an article or flagging a gap. This produces a reliable signal about which issue categories are underserved by the KB.

Gap flags trigger an article creation SLA. The SLA varies by the tier that flagged the gap, because a gap flagged by L3 (a model safety runbook that does not exist) is more urgent than one flagged by L1 (a usage question that could have been answered by better documentation). A gap that accumulates more than three flags in a 14-day window is automatically promoted to high priority, regardless of the tier that flagged it, because frequency signals customer impact.
Integrating KB Search into the Ticket Workflow

A knowledge base that agents must navigate to in a separate tab will not be used consistently. KB lookup must be embedded in the ticket interface so the top 3–5 article matches appear automatically when a ticket is opened, based on the issue category and the first 50–100 words of the ticket body. The agent reviews the suggestions before composing a response. If one resolves the ticket, they select it; the system links the article to the ticket and increments the article's help_rate counter.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Protocol

class KBSearchBackend(Protocol):
    def search(self, query: str, tier: str, limit: int) -> list[dict]:
        """Returns list of {article_id, title, relevance_score} dicts."""
        ...

@dataclass
class TicketKBSuggestions:
    ticket_id: str
    ticket_body: str
    assigned_tier: str
    search_backend: KBSearchBackend

    def suggest(self, limit: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
        # Build query from ticket body (first 100 words) + tier filter
        words = self.ticket_body.split()[:100]
        query = " ".join(words)
        results = self.search_backend.search(query, self.assigned_tier, limit)
        return [r for r in results if r["relevance_score"] >= 0.4]

    def record_outcome(self, article_id: str | None, helped: bool) -> None:
        # Called at ticket close
        # article_id: the article the agent selected (None if none selected)
        # helped: True if article contributed to resolution
        if article_id is None:
            # flag KB gap for this issue category
            return
        # increment times_surfaced on all suggested articles
        # increment times_helped_resolve on article_id if helped=True
        # increment times_flagged_unhelpful on article_id if helped=False

The escalation and KB integration program has two health signals: escalation rate and KB deflection rate. Escalation rate is the percentage of L1 tickets that are escalated to L2, and L2 to L3. High escalation rates at L1 indicate either that the KB is not covering the current issue distribution, or that L1 agents are under-trained for the resolution steps the tier expects them to perform. Deflection rate is the percentage of tickets where a KB article was surfaced and the agent marked it as helpful. High deflection rates mean the KB is working — agents are resolving issues without escalating.

These three metrics — L1 escalation rate, L2 escalation rate, and KB deflection rate — are reviewed in the monthly support program health review. The target thresholds (L1 escalation < 30%, L2 escalation < 15%, KB deflection > 40%) are not aspirational; they represent the steady-state operating levels of a functioning tier model. Sustained breach of any threshold triggers a root-cause investigation before the next review cycle, not after it.

Escalation flows upward by necessity; de-escalation should flow downward by design. When L3 diagnoses a model behavior issue and determines that the root cause is a prompt engineering problem rather than a model defect, the ticket should be returned to L2 with a clear explanation and the relevant KB article linked. When L2 concludes that an issue a customer described as a systematic failure is actually a single-request anomaly, it should be returned to L1 for closure. De-escalation keeps the higher tiers focused on work that genuinely requires their capabilities.

De-escalation must include a written explanation in the ticket that the customer sees. A ticket that moves from L3 back to L1 without explanation looks like the customer's issue was lost in a handoff. The note should explain what was found, why the issue does not require engineering escalation, and what the recommended next step is. This transparency maintains customer trust even when the diagnosis is "this was a usage issue, not a product defect."

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class DeescalationReason(str, Enum):
    PROMPT_ENGINEERING_ISSUE     = "prompt_engineering_issue"
    SINGLE_REQUEST_ANOMALY       = "single_request_anomaly"
    DOCUMENTATION_RESOLUTION     = "documentation_resolution"
    CUSTOMER_ENVIRONMENT_FACTOR  = "customer_environment_factor"
    MODEL_BEHAVIOUR_AS_DESIGNED  = "model_behaviour_as_designed"

@dataclass
class DeescalationPacket:
    ticket_id: str
    from_tier: str           # "L3" or "L2"
    to_tier: str             # "L2" or "L1"
    reason: DeescalationReason
    findings_summary: str    # what was found; surfaces in customer-visible note
    recommended_action: str  # what L2/L1 should do next
    kb_article_id: str | None  # link to relevant article, if one exists

    def customer_note(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"Our team reviewed this in depth. Findings: {self.findings_summary}. "
            f"Recommended next step: {self.recommended_action}."
        )
Escalating to avoid a difficult conversation:

When L1 escalates because a customer is frustrated, not because the issue is outside L1 scope, the tier model breaks. L2 receives a ticket that is already resolved at the documentation level but carries an angry customer context. L1 must be trained to de-escalate frustration within their tier before routing the technical issue.

If the KB article for the issue exists and the agent did not check it before escalating, the KB deflection system failed. Ticket close forms must require agents to confirm they searched the KB and list the articles checked. If zero articles are listed, the quality review flag fires.
L2 holding tickets pending customer response indefinitely:

Tickets awaiting customer reply should be moved to a "pending customer" state with an auto-close timer (14 days for L2, 7 days for L1). Tickets that sit in the active queue waiting for customer input inflate queue depth metrics and obscure true workload. The customer should receive a reminder at day 3 and day 7, then auto-close with a note that they can reopen.
Treating the KB as a read-only archive:

Agents who never flag gaps or mark articles unhelpful are producing a false signal of KB health. If help rates are uniformly high but escalation rates are also high, agents are not using the KB at all — they are closing the suggestion panel and escalating. Manager review of agent KB interaction patterns is necessary to distinguish genuine KB health from non-use.

Every week, the support EM reviews a sample of tickets that were escalated from L1 to L2 and from L2 to L3. The review asks four questions: Was the escalation necessary given the tier routing rules? Was the escalation packet complete when it arrived at the receiving tier? Did the escalation add significant resolution time compared to tickets the receiving tier owned from the start? Was a KB article updated or created as a result of the escalation?

These retrospectives are the feedback loop for the escalation protocol itself. If a recurring pattern shows that L1 escalates a particular issue category at a high rate but L2 resolves them quickly using an existing KB article, that article is not being surfaced at L1 effectively — either the search query construction is wrong or the article's tier_audience field does not include L1. The retro turns operational data into protocol changes rather than just reporting numbers.
Check KB coverage for the issue category; update article or add L1 resolution step
Flag the escalating agent for training; update the escalation form to require missing fields
Review whether L2 could have been skipped — is this a candidate for L1 → L3 direct path?
Was a KB article created or updated?
Create the article gap ticket; assign under KB creation SLA

AI application support requires a class of KB article that does not exist in conventional software support: the model behavior runbook. Standard KB articles describe how to configure a product or resolve an error code — information that is stable and general. Model behavior runbooks address situations where the model's output is the source of the support request, and the diagnosis depends on understanding how specific inputs interact with model behavior at a technical level.

Model behavior runbooks are authored by L3 engineers after a confirmed model-layer escalation. They are structured differently from documentation articles: they describe the specific input conditions that trigger the behavior, the expected versus actual output, and the recommended response — which is almost always a prompt modification or a temperature/sampling parameter change rather than a product bug fix. These runbooks are restricted to L2 and L3 audiences because applying them incorrectly can produce worse output than the original complaint.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class ModelBehaviorRunbook:
    runbook_id: str
    title: str
    model_versions_affected: list[str]   # e.g. ["claude-3-5-sonnet", "claude-3-opus"]
    trigger_conditions: str              # description of inputs that trigger the behavior
    expected_output: str
    observed_output: str
    diagnostic_steps: list[str]         # ordered; L2 follows these before escalating to L3
    recommended_fix: str                 # prompt change, param change, or escalate
    tier_audience: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: ["L2", "L3"])
    requires_l3_review: bool = False     # True for safety-adjacent behaviors

    def is_applicable(self, model_version: str) -> bool:
        return any(v in model_version for v in self.model_versions_affected)

    def diagnostic_checklist(self) -> str:
        return "\n".join(f"{i+1}. {step}" for i, step in enumerate(self.diagnostic_steps))


# Example: runbook for instruction-following regression at high context length
long_context_runbook = ModelBehaviorRunbook(
    runbook_id="RB-0041",
    title="Instruction Following Degradation at P95 Context Length",
    model_versions_affected=["claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"],
    trigger_conditions="System prompt > 8,000 tokens AND user turn > 2,000 tokens. "                       "Model may de-prioritise later instructions in the system prompt.",
    expected_output="Complies with all system prompt instructions including those near end of prompt.",
    observed_output="Follows instructions at start of system prompt; ignores or partially ignores "                    "instructions placed after the 6,000-token mark.",
    diagnostic_steps=[
        "Reproduce with customer's exact system prompt and a minimal user turn (< 100 tokens)",
        "Verify the ignored instruction is after token 6,000 in the system prompt",
        "Test with instruction moved to the first 500 tokens of the system prompt",
        "Test with a shorter, semantically equivalent rewrite of the instruction",
    ],
    recommended_fix="Move critical instructions to the first 500 tokens of the system prompt. "                    "If not possible due to customer's architecture, escalate to L3 for evaluation.",
    requires_l3_review=False,
)

Every confirmed L3 model escalation must produce a runbook or update an existing one. This is the mechanism by which L3 engineering effort compounds: the first occurrence of a behavior class is a full investigation; subsequent occurrences are a 30-minute L2 diagnosis and a recommendation, because the runbook exists. Without this obligation, L3 handles the same class of issue from scratch every time it appears in a new customer context.

Each article in the knowledge base has a designated owner by tier. L1 owns articles that cover authentication, billing, account management, and basic API usage. L2 owns articles that cover prompt engineering, API parameter tuning, performance optimisation, and known integration patterns. L3 owns model behavior runbooks and safety-adjacent diagnostic guides. Ownership means the owner is responsible for keeping the article current — they receive the freshness alert when the article crosses the staleness threshold, and they are the named reviewer for updates authored by others.

Cross-tier ownership creates a specific risk: when L3 engineers are fully occupied with production incidents, model behavior runbooks go stale because the L3 owner does not have capacity to review them. This is mitigated by two mechanisms: model behavior runbooks have a freshness threshold of 60 days rather than 90 (because model versions change more frequently than product behavior), and a backup owner from the L2 technical team is designated for each runbook, with authority to make minor updates without waiting for L3 review. Major updates — changes to the trigger conditions or the recommended fix — always require L3 sign-off.
L3 for major; L2 backup for minor

Escalation and knowledge base integration are the mechanisms that prevent a three-tier support model from collapsing into a single-tier queue. Structured escalation packets — with reproduction steps, KB articles checked, and customer impact summaries — ensure that receiving tiers do not re-gather context the escalating tier already had. The knowledge base feedback loop, driven by gap flags and article help-rate tracking, keeps the article library aligned with the actual question distribution rather than drifting toward historical issues. Program health is measured by escalation rate and KB deflection rate, both reviewed monthly against defined thresholds.
